title: Introduction to Joins
content:
### Introduction to SQL Joins

Joins combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column between them. They are fundamental to working with relational databases.

---

### Why Use Joins?

Relational databases store data in separate tables to avoid redundancy. Joins allow you to reconstruct complete data by linking related tables.

### Sample Tables

```sql
-- employees table
id | name       | dept_id
1  | Alice      | 1
2  | Bob        | 2
3  | Charlie    | 1

-- departments table
id | dept_name
1  | Engineering
2  | Sales
```

### Types of Joins

| Join Type | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| INNER JOIN | Returns matching rows from both tables |
| LEFT JOIN | Returns all rows from left table, matching from right |
| RIGHT JOIN | Returns all rows from right table, matching from left |
| FULL OUTER JOIN | Returns all rows when there is a match in either table |
| CROSS JOIN | Returns Cartesian product of both tables |
| SELF JOIN | Joins a table with itself |
| NATURAL JOIN | Joins using columns with the same name |

### ON vs USING

```sql
-- ON: specify any condition
SELECT * FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id;

-- USING: join when columns have the same name
SELECT * FROM employees
JOIN departments USING (dept_id);
```

> **Key Point:** Joins are typically performed on foreign key relationships. The ON clause specifies how tables are related.
codeExample:
SELECT e.name AS employee_name,
       d.dept_name AS department
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id;

quiz.question: Why are joins necessary in relational databases?
options: To combine data from related tables that are stored separately

quiz.question: Which clause specifies how tables are related in a join?
